codeReview.summary: The seed data expansion is correctly implemented. The single file prisma/seed.ts was modified with +1081/-16 lines, expanding from 6 products + 12 events to 30 products + 42 events. All 6 stable product IDs (product-stable-001 through 006) and 12 stable event IDs (event-stable-001 through 012) are preserved unchanged with identical data. Products span 5 categories (14 Football Jersey, 6 Scarf, 4 Cap, 3 Memorabilia, 3 Accessories) with 18 distinct price points ($9.99-$49.99). Events have exactly 14 per type (football, basketball, concert) with 37 distinct price points ($68-$349.99). All 30 product imageUrls use flagcdn.com with unique country codes (30 distinct country codes, no duplicates). Events use emoji flags for football/basketball and empty string artistImageUrl for concerts, matching the existing pattern. Date ranges span May-September 2026 with varied venues and listing intelligence flag combinations.

issues.description: All 6 Scarf products share the same price ($15.99) and all 4 Cap products share from only 2 price points ($24.99 and $19.99). While the overall feature meets the 5+ distinct price points requirement (18 distinct), having more price variation within non-jersey categories would improve data realism for UI testing of price filters or sorting.

The worker skill (platform-worker) specifies step 2 'Write tests first (TDD)' and step 5 'Interactive verification with agent-browser'. Since this feature only modifies seed data and doesn't add new code paths, skipping TDD and agent-browser verification was reasonable. No new tests were needed; all 354 existing tests pass.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker initially introduced duplicate product imageUrls across categories (scarves, caps, etc. reusing the same country code flags as jerseys). This was caught during API verification and required a second pass of ~16 individual edits to assign unique country codes. The stable-seed-ids.md library file documents the 6+12 stable IDs but doesn't mention the unique-image-per-product constraint. Adding this constraint to the library or AGENTS.md image strategy section would prevent future workers from making the same mistake.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows worker first seeded, then verified with jq duplicate check, found duplicates, then made ~16 individual edits to fix country codes. The 'Image Strategy' section in AGENTS.md mentions flagcdn pattern but not the uniqueness constraint.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The stable-seed-ids.md library file should be updated to reflect that there are now 30 products and 42 events total, not just the 6+12 stable ones. Future workers referencing this file may not realize the expanded dataset exists. Consider noting the total counts and category distribution.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: stable-seed-ids.md only documents the 6 stable products and 12 stable events. The 24 new products and 30 new events are undocumented.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker skill says 'Write tests first (TDD)' as step 2, but for seed-data-only changes, TDD is not applicable. The skill procedure doesn't distinguish between features that add new code (API, components) vs. data-only changes. The worker correctly skipped TDD (followedProcedure=true in handoff, which seems incorrect — TDD was skipped), but the skill could be clearer about when TDD applies.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Skill SKILL.md step 2: 'Write tests first (TDD)'. Handoff: skillFeedback.followedProcedure=true with no deviations noted, despite TDD being skipped. No new tests were added.

summary: PASS. Seed data expansion correctly implemented in a single file (prisma/seed.ts). 30 products across 5 categories with 18 distinct price points and 30 unique flagcdn.com images. 42 events (14 per type) with 37 distinct event price points, varied dates (May-Sep 2026), venues, and listing intelligence flags. All 18 stable IDs preserved unchanged. Only file modified was prisma/seed.ts. All validators passed (354 tests, typecheck, lint). Two non-blocking observations: scarves have uniform pricing ($15.99), and the worker initially created duplicate images but self-corrected during verification.
